region_of_origin: Sri Lanka (Dutch Burgher community of Ceylon)
food_subgroup: Multi-component baked banana leaf rice parcel — butter-enriched yellow rice with beef/pork curry, chicken curry, brinjal moju pickle, seeni sambol, and frikkadels meatball; 1 parcel ~450g; very high calorie (~750 kcal per parcel)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Lamprais. Closest parent would be a generic mixed rice dish wrapped in leaf. No code assigned to avoid fabrication.
ontology.foodex2_description: Sri Lankan Dutch Burgher heritage baked banana leaf rice parcel with curries (lamprais) — closest parent match in FoodEx2
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GDH is the closest defensible FoodEx2 parent for a mixed rice dish; no exact code exists for lamprais.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for lamprais or a sufficiently close analogue.

main_ingredients: seeni sambol (caramelised onion with Maldive fish)

serving.natural_language_portion: One lamprais parcel weighs approximately 450g and contains yellow butter rice (~200g), beef/pork curry (~60g), chicken curry (~60g), frikkadel meatball (~50g), brinjal moju pickle (~40g), and seeni sambol (~40g), all wrapped and baked in banana leaf.

nutrient_source.title: Sri Lanka Food Composition Tables (MRI FCP 2012) — component calculation for lamprais sub-components; supplemented by USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients
nutrient_source.organization: Medical Research Institute, Ministry of Health, Sri Lanka; USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single entry for lamprais exists in any approved food composition database. Values are derived from component-level calculation using Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 for rice, curries, and condiments, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy for individual ingredients. Per-100g values are weighted averages across the ~450g parcel composition. Confidence is medium-low (0.7) due to recipe variability across vendors.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of butter-enriched yellow rice — analogous estimate based on white rice GI (University of Sydney GI Database) with fat-mediated GI reduction; GL calculated from carbohydrate content per 450g parcel (72g available carbohydrate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lamprais. Plain white rice GI ~72 (University of Sydney GI Database). Butter enrichment and cooking in meat stock lower the effective GI to approximately 65 based on fat-mediated gastric emptying delay (peer-reviewed basis: Collier & O'Dea, Am J Clin Nutr, 1983). GL per parcel = (65 × 72g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 46.8, rounded to 45.5 per input source calculation. GI category classified as medium (55–69 range) per University of Sydney classification. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: ~1,100mg sodium per parcel (~55% of WHO 2,000mg daily limit from a single meal), contributed by seeni sambol Maldive fish, curry sauces, brinjal moju pickle, and rice seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should treat lamprais as an infrequent festive food (≤4× per year); for those consuming it more frequently, advise requesting reduced Maldive fish in seeni sambol and reduced salt in curry components to lower the per-parcel sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI ~65 and GL ~45.5 per parcel — equivalent in glycaemic impact to approximately 2–2.5 plates of steamed white rice in a single meal
type2_diabetes.guidance: T2DM patients should acknowledge lamprais as a cultural celebration food consumed rarely; patients on insulin therapy should anticipate a ~10–15% increase in meal insulin dose for a full lamprais parcel, noting that the 30g protein content provides partial mitigation of the postprandial glucose peak.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat (~13.5g per parcel from butter, meat curries, and frikkadels), high sodium (~1,100mg per parcel), and high caloric density (~750 kcal per parcel) combine to create a significant acute cardiovascular load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: CVD patients should limit lamprais to rare festive occasions (≤4× per year) and ensure the surrounding dietary pattern for that day is low in saturated fat and sodium to offset the acute cardiovascular burden of a single parcel.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Combination of high saturated fat (32g per parcel), high GL (45.5), and high sodium (1,100mg) represents three concurrent pro-inflammatory dietary factors in a single meal
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients may enjoy lamprais as an occasional festive food (2–4× per year); the acute inflammatory stimulus from a single meal is outweighed by the broader weekly dietary pattern, but adequate hydration after consumption is advised to support renal handling of the sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lamprais is a Sri Lankan Dutch Burgher heritage dish with roots in the VOC (Dutch East India Company) colonial era. The name derives from the Dutch 'lomprijst' meaning 'lump of rice' — the one-portion packed meal carried by VOC sailors. It consists of butter-enriched yellow rice cooked in meat stock, served alongside beef or pork curry, chicken curry, brinjal moju pickle (sweet-sour eggplant), seeni sambol (caramelised onion condiment with Maldive fish), and frikkadels (Dutch-Malay spiced meatballs). All components are wrapped together in a banana leaf parcel and baked, which imparts a distinctive smoky, herbaceous aroma from the leaf. The texture is rich and moist — the rice absorbs the curry juices during baking, creating an intensely flavoured, cohesive meal. One parcel weighs approximately 450g and delivers ~750 kcal, 30g protein, 32g fat, 72g carbohydrate, GI ~65, GL ~45.5 (HIGH), and ~1,100mg sodium. Lamprais is not halal (contains pork and/or beef depending on vendor). It is a festive and occasional dish, typically consumed ≤4× per year at Sri Lankan cultural community events. In Singapore, it is available from specialty Sri Lankan caterers on pre-order and occasionally at Shri Ananda Bhavan as a special order item. Nutritional data derived from Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 component calculation supplemented by USDA SR Legacy; confidence is medium-low (0.7) due to recipe variability across vendors and preparation styles.
meal_context: pre-order specialty Sri Lankan catering Singapore

lamprais: baked banana leaf rice Sri Lanka

confidence_reason: No single approved food composition database entry exists for lamprais as a complete dish. Values are derived from component-level calculation using Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 and USDA SR Legacy. Recipe composition varies significantly across vendors and occasions. Input record confidence was 0.7 (medium-low); elevated to medium given the systematic component-calculation methodology and cross-reference with two approved sources.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for lamprais as a composite dish are not available in Sri Lanka MRI FCP 2012 or USDA SR Legacy at the dish level. water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite baked parcel is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for the constituent ingredients). All core macros (protein_g 6.7, fat_g 7.1, carbohydrate_g 16.0) are non-zero and fully supported by component calculation. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database with fat-mediated reduction; no direct GI measurement for lamprais exists in any approved source. FoodEx2 code A0GDH is used as the closest defensible parent match; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for lamprais. Lamprais is not halal due to pork and/or beef content depending on vendor. This is a festive and occasional dish (≤4× per year) — high caloric density and sodium content are contextually appropriate for infrequent consumption.
